How many mutations are enough to rule linkage out?

Author

Hunter Ries

Introduction

When two pathogen genomes are separated by k mutations, how confident can we be that the cases are directly linked by transmission? This document explores that question by simulating pairs of linked and un-linked cases and computing the empirical probability P(directly linked | k mutations) for each observed mutation count.

The key insight is asymmetric: a small number of mutations cannot confirm linkage (many un-linked pairs also differ by few mutations when evolutionary rates are low), but a large number of mutations can effectively rule linkage out. This is because directly linked pairs are constrained by the serial interval, while un-linked pairs accumulate mutations over much longer divergence times.

This document recapitulates Figures 3.5 and 3.6 from Alli Black’s Applied Genomic Epidemiology Handbook §3.7.1, generalized so that pathogen parameters can be swapped easily via a single editable table.

Setup

library(tidyverse)
set.seed(920674206)

Pathogen parameters

Edit this table to add or modify pathogens. Each row drives one simulation and plot.

pathogens <- tibble(
  name              = c("pandemic H1N1",
                        "SARS-CoV-2",
                        "West Nile virus",
                        "Mpox clade IIb",
                        "HIV-1 (pol region)",
                        "Influenza A H3N2"),
  mu_subs_site_year = c(0.003406, 0.0008, 0.0005, 0.00006, 0.0025, 0.0045),
  genome_length_nt  = c(13154,    29903,  11000,  197000,   9200,   13500),
  si_mean_days      = c(3.0,      5.0,    14.0,   9.0,      60.0,   3.0),
  si_sd_days        = c(1.0,      1.5,    4.0,    3.0,      30.0,   1.0)
)
# NOTE: West Nile virus is vector-borne — "serial interval" is a loose
# approximation (vector–host cycle time varies widely). Interpret WNV plots
# with caution.
#
# NOTE: HIV-1 is a chronic infection with highly variable time to onward
# transmission. The "serial interval" here is a rough placeholder; the
# resulting plots should be treated as illustrative only.

Simulation function

#' Simulate linked and unlinked genome pairs and compute P(linked | k)
#'
#' @param mu Substitution rate (subs/site/year)
#' @param L Genome length (nt)
#' @param si_mean Mean serial interval (days)
#' @param si_sd SD of serial interval (days)
#' @param n_pairs Number of pairs to simulate per class per replicate
#' @param n_reps Number of independent replicates (each yields its own
#'   P(linked|k) estimate, creating vertical spread in the plot)
#' @param unlinked_multiplier_range Range of multipliers on si_mean for
#'   unlinked divergence times
#' @return A tibble with columns rep, k, prob_linked_given_k
simulate_linkage <- function(mu, L, si_mean, si_sd,
                             n_pairs = 1000,
                             n_reps = 200,
                             unlinked_multiplier_range = c(5, 50)) {

  # Gamma parameters for the serial interval (in days)
  shape <- (si_mean / si_sd)^2
  rate  <- si_mean / si_sd^2

  map_dfr(seq_len(n_reps), function(r) {

    # --- Linked pairs ---
    tau_linked    <- rgamma(n_pairs, shape = shape, rate = rate)
    lambda_linked <- mu * L * (tau_linked / 365.25)
    k_linked      <- rpois(n_pairs, lambda = lambda_linked)

    # --- Unlinked pairs ---
    tau_unlinked    <- runif(
      n_pairs,
      min = unlinked_multiplier_range[1] * si_mean,
      max = unlinked_multiplier_range[2] * si_mean
    )
    lambda_unlinked <- mu * L * (tau_unlinked / 365.25)
    k_unlinked      <- rpois(n_pairs, lambda = lambda_unlinked)

    # --- P(linked | k) for this replicate ---
    n_linked_k   <- table(factor(k_linked))
    n_unlinked_k <- table(factor(k_unlinked))
    all_k        <- union(names(n_linked_k), names(n_unlinked_k))

    tibble(
      k = as.integer(all_k),
      n_linked   = as.integer(n_linked_k[all_k]),
      n_unlinked = as.integer(n_unlinked_k[all_k])
    ) |>
      replace_na(list(n_linked = 0L, n_unlinked = 0L)) |>
      mutate(
        prob_linked_given_k = n_linked / (n_linked + n_unlinked),
        rep = r
      ) |>
      select(rep, k, prob_linked_given_k)
  })
}

Plotting function

#' Plot P(directly linked | k mutations) for linked-pair samples
#'
#' @param sim_df Output of simulate_linkage()
#' @param title Plot title
#' @param subtitle Plot subtitle
#' @param k_min_display Minimum k value shown on x-axis (default 5)
#' @return A ggplot object
plot_linkage <- function(sim_df, title, subtitle, k_min_display = 5) {

  # Show at least k_min_display on x-axis
  n_reps  <- n_distinct(sim_df$rep)
  k_rep_counts <- sim_df |> count(k) # number of reps that produced this k
  k_max_data   <- max(
    k_rep_counts$k[k_rep_counts$n >= 0.25 * n_reps],
    na.rm = TRUE
  )
  # Override k_max_data
  k_max_data <- k_min_display
  k_max <- max(k_min_display, k_max_data)

  plot_df <- sim_df |> filter(k <= k_max)

  ggplot(plot_df, aes(x = k, y = prob_linked_given_k)) +
    geom_violin(
      aes(group = k),
      fill = "grey80", colour = NA, alpha = 0.5,
      scale = "width", width = 0.7
    ) +
    geom_jitter(
      width = 0.2, height = 0,
      alpha = 0.15, size = 0.5, colour = "steelblue"
    ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = 0.05, linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey40") +
    annotate(
      "text", x = k_max * 0.85, y = 0.08,
      label = "5% rule-out threshold",
      size = 3, colour = "grey40"
    ) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = seq(0, k_max, by = 1)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, 1), labels = scales::percent) +
    labs(
      x        = "Mutations separating consensus genomes",
      y        = "P(directly linked)",
      title    = title,
      subtitle = subtitle
    ) +
    theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
    theme(panel.grid.minor = element_blank())
}

Results

pwalk(pathogens, function(name, mu_subs_site_year, genome_length_nt,
                          si_mean_days, si_sd_days) {
  sim <- simulate_linkage(
    mu      = mu_subs_site_year,
    L       = genome_length_nt,
    si_mean = si_mean_days,
    si_sd   = si_sd_days
  )

  subtitle <- sprintf(
    "\u03bc = %g subs/site/yr \u00b7 L = %s nt \u00b7 SI = %.1f \u00b1 %.1f days",
    mu_subs_site_year,
    format(genome_length_nt, big.mark = ","),
    si_mean_days,
    si_sd_days
  )

  p <- plot_linkage(sim, title = name, subtitle = subtitle)
  print(p)
})

Modeling caveats

  • The 50/50 prior on linkage is implicit in pooling equal numbers of linked and un-linked simulated pairs. Real outbreak datasets have different mixing ratios, and the posterior probability shifts accordingly.
  • Within-host diversity, transmission bottlenecks, and the fact that mutations accumulate on both sides of the MRCA are all collapsed into a single Poisson draw per pair.
  • Serial interval ≠ generation interval; we are conflating them here for simplicity.
  • For vector-borne pathogens (West Nile virus) and chronic infections (HIV), the “serial interval” framing breaks down. Treat those plots as illustrative only.
  • A future version should add an analytic Bayesian solution (gamma–Poisson conjugacy → negative binomial marginal) with an explicit, tunable prior on linkage.
